entries.value: Do not change the toggle’s label based on its state
If there is additional information about the setting, it should be provided as a caption underneath the control
A ToggleSwitch must have a clear, descriptive, and permanently visible label so that users understand the purpose of the input
Switches should be used instead of radio buttons if each item in a set can be independently controlled
Switches can be on or off. Switches have enabled, disabled, hover, focused, and pressed states.
Toggles have six states: on, off, focus, disabled, skeleton, and read-only.
Let people know what happens when the toggle is switched by using a tooltip.
Toggles should never require users to press a button to apply the settings.
When writing tooltips for toggle states, be short and concise
A toggle is used to view or switch between enabled or disabled states.
A toggle is used to quickly switch between two possible states. They are commonly used for “on/off” switches
Switches toggle the selection of an item on or off
Switches allow users to turn an individual option on or off. They are usually used to activate or deactivate a specific setting.
ToggleSwitch is used to immediately toggle a setting on or off.
A switch lets the user toggle an individual setting on or off.
Use switches to turn an item on or off, especially on mobile instead of a checkbox.
Do keep the label consistent. Only change the state text between On and Off.
Use a neutral tone - Because switches are used for controls and utility, their labels are written in a neutral, utilitarian way. There’s no need for overly celebratory language.
Use succinct phrases with verbs or nouns
Use a ToggleSwitch like a button
Don't use radio buttons to toggle items on or off. Radio buttons convey that a set of items are options, and that only one can be selected at a time. Instead, use switches.
Do not change the label text as a way of indicating the toggle's on/off state.
No partial state - Switches can only be on or off.
Avoid using verb phrases related to a state of activity - such as “turn on” or “turn off.”
Don't use phrases that describe the switch's state
Don't use a ToggleSwitch as a replacement for a checkbox
